exploit_chains.name: Physical Memory Attack
steps: 1. Use phys-rw (IOCTL 0x81112F08 [phys mem map], 0x81112F1C [phys mem map], 0x81112F5C [phys mem map] +2 more) to scan physical memory for EPROCESS signatures
2. Locate current process EPROCESS in physical memory
3. Overwrite Token field with SYSTEM process token
4. Alternative: map ntoskrnl .text, patch code inline

findings.title: Dangerous import: IoCreateSymbolicLink
findings.description: Creates symbolic link — check accessibility

findings.title: Dangerous import: IoDeleteSymbolicLink
findings.description: Deletes symbolic link — racy deletion enables stale-link hijack

findings.title: Dangerous import: MmMapLockedPagesSpecifyCache
findings.description: Maps locked pages with cache control

findings.title: Dangerous import: MmMapIoSpace
findings.description: Maps physical memory — arbitrary R/W primitive if address is user-controlled

findings.title: Dangerous import: MmGetSystemRoutineAddress
findings.description: Resolves kernel export by name — used in DSE bypass to find CI!g_CiOptions

findings.title: Dangerous import: IoCreateDevice
findings.description: Creates device object — check if SecurityDescriptor is NULL

findings.title: Dangerous import: ZwSetValueKey
findings.description: Writes registry values from kernel

findings.title: Dangerous import: ZwCreateKey
findings.description: Creates registry keys from kernel

findings.title: Dangerous import: ExAllocatePoolWithTag
findings.description: Pool allocation — check for size validation

findings.title: No ProbeForRead/ProbeForWrite imports found
findings.description: Driver imports dangerous APIs but never imports ProbeForRead or ProbeForWrite. User buffer validation is likely missing.

findings.title: WDF (Windows Driver Framework) driver detected
findings.description: Driver uses WDF framework (detected imports: WdfVersionBind). IOCTL dispatch uses EvtIoDeviceControl callback via WdfIoQueueCreate rather than DriverObject->MajorFunction[].

findings.title: Hardcoded address: 0xE0000
findings.description: Hardcoded page-aligned address found. May be a physical memory address used with MmMapIoSpace or similar.

findings.title: Hardcoded address: 0xE0000
findings.description: Hardcoded page-aligned address found. May be a physical memory address used with MmMapIoSpace or similar.

findings.title: Integer overflow before ExAllocatePoolWithTag at 0x140006362
findings.description: Arithmetic (imul/shl/mul) at 0x14000634F precedes ExAllocatePoolWithTag call at 0x140006362 with no overflow guard (no jo/jb between them). If the multiplied value is user-controlled, this causes a heap overflow.

findings.title: Integer overflow before ExAllocatePoolWithTag at 0x14002DADD
findings.description: Arithmetic (imul/shl/mul) at 0x14002DAC6 precedes ExAllocatePoolWithTag call at 0x14002DADD with no overflow guard (no jo/jb between them). If the multiplied value is user-controlled, this causes a heap overflow.

findings.title: Integer overflow before ExAllocatePoolWithTag at 0x14002DD53
findings.description: Arithmetic (imul/shl/mul) at 0x14002DD3F precedes ExAllocatePoolWithTag call at 0x14002DD53 with no overflow guard (no jo/jb between them). If the multiplied value is user-controlled, this causes a heap overflow.

findings.title: DKOM: EPROCESS list walk pattern (Win10_1507 offsets)
findings.description: Driver accesses EPROCESS ActiveProcessLinks (0x2f0) and UniqueProcessId (0x2e8), matching Win10_1507 layout. This suggests EPROCESS enumeration (process hiding or targeting).

findings.title: Double-fetch (TOCTOU) at 0x14000154E
findings.description: Two reads from [reg+0x18] at 0x1400014A4 and 0x14000154E with a cmp/test using the first value and the second value flowing into a sensitive sink (allocator / memcpy / deref). An attacker can race to change the buffer between check and use.

findings.title: Double-fetch (TOCTOU) at 0x1400016C0
findings.description: Two reads from [reg+0x4] at 0x140001634 and 0x1400016C0 with a cmp/test using the first value and the second value flowing into a sensitive sink (allocator / memcpy / deref). An attacker can race to change the buffer between check and use.

findings.title: Double-fetch (TOCTOU) at 0x140002102
findings.description: Two reads from [reg+0x10] at 0x140002080 and 0x140002102 with a cmp/test using the first value and the second value flowing into a sensitive sink (allocator / memcpy / deref). An attacker can race to change the buffer between check and use.

findings.title: Double-fetch (TOCTOU) at 0x140003FD3
findings.description: Two reads from [reg+0x3] at 0x140003F36 and 0x140003FD3 with a cmp/test using the first value and the second value flowing into a sensitive sink (allocator / memcpy / deref). An attacker can race to change the buffer between check and use.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: Signature enforcement (FORCE_INTEGRITY)
findings.description: Signature enforcement (FORCE_INTEGRITY) is not enabled. Driver can load without a valid Authenticode signature.

findings.title: Driver is NOT HVCI-compatible
findings.description: Driver fails HVCI checks (missing FORCE_INTEGRITY or has W+X sections). Cannot load in HVCI-enabled environments without policy bypass.

findings.title: Kernel write gadgets found (8 instances)
findings.description: Multiple 'mov [reg+offset], reg' patterns in driver code. If the register is derived from user input (e.g., IOCTL buffer), this is an arbitrary kernel memory write primitive.

details.gadgets: 0x140001000: mov dword ptr [rsp + 0x10], edx; 0x140001004: mov qword ptr [rsp + 8], rcx; 0x140001031: mov dword ptr [rsp + 4], eax; 0x140001064: mov qword ptr [rsp + 0x18], r8

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'phys mem map' at 0x1400010EC
findings.description: Function at 0x1400010EC performs 'phys mem map' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: No PreviousMode / RequestorMode check detected
findings.description: Driver does not import ExGetPreviousMode or KeGetPreviousMode and no inline KTHREAD.PreviousMode read was found. Without checking PreviousMode, a kernel-mode caller could be impersonated, or user-mode requests may bypass validation. IOCTLs should verify RequestorMode == UserMode before trusting input.

findings.title: IOCTL handlers missing InputBufferLength validation
findings.description: The following IOCTL handlers do not appear to validate InputBufferLength before processing the buffer: 0x46414350, 0x52534454, 0x58534454, 0x80000005. Without size checks, a short buffer could cause pool overread or out-of-bounds writes.

findings.title: IOCTL handlers missing NULL buffer check
findings.description: These IOCTL handlers may not check for NULL SystemBuffer before dereferencing: 0x81112EE0, 0x81112EE4, 0x81112F48, 0x81112F68, 0x81112F6C, 0x81112F70, 0x81112F74, 0x81112F78, 0x81112F7C, 0x81112F84, 0x81112F8C, 0x81112F90, 0x81112FA8, 0x81112FAC, 0x81112FB0, 0x81112FD0, 0x81112FD4, 0x81112FD8, 0x81112FF8. A zero-length IOCTL request can result in SystemBuffer=NULL, causing a kernel NULL-pointer dereference (BSOD).

findings.title: No privilege check APIs imported
findings.description: Driver handles IOCTLs but does not import SeSinglePrivilegeCheck, SePrivilegeCheck, or SeAccessCheck. Sensitive operations should verify caller privileges beyond just device handle access rights.

findings.title: IoCreateDevice without FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN
findings.description: Driver uses IoCreateDevice instead of IoCreateDeviceSecure and FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag was not detected. Without this flag, the device object inherits permissive default security, allowing any user to open a handle. Use IoCreateDeviceSecure with an SDDL string to restrict access.

findings.title: Driver has Authenticode signature
findings.description: PE contains a WIN_CERTIFICATE structure with embedded signature.

findings.title: Signed by: Advanced Micro Devices INC.
findings.description:
Subject: Advanced Micro Devices INC. (Advanced Micro Devices INC.)
Issuer: Symantec Class 3 SHA256 Code Signing CA
Serial: 1885b7e188d8fafd38a43d48967d7488
Key: RSA 2048-bit
Valid until: 2022-02-13T23:59:59+00:00

details.issuer: Symantec Class 3 SHA256 Code Signing CA

findings.title: Signing certificate EXPIRED (valid until 2022-02-13T23:59:59+00:00)
findings.description: The code-signing certificate has expired. While drivers with a valid timestamp counter-signature remain valid after expiry, an expired cert without a timestamp means the signature is no longer trusted. This is also common in BYOVD — old vulnerable drivers with expired certs that were signed before revocation.

findings.title: Expired certificates in chain: Symantec Time Stamping Services CA - G2, Symantec Time Stamping Services Signer - G4, Symantec Class 3 SHA256 Code Signing CA, VeriSign Class 3 Public Primary Certification Authority - G5
findings.description: One or more certificates in the signing chain have expired. If the root or intermediate CA cert has expired, the entire chain of trust is broken unless a valid timestamp exists.

expired_certs: Symantec Time Stamping Services CA - G2
Symantec Time Stamping Services Signer - G4
Symantec Class 3 SHA256 Code Signing CA
VeriSign Class 3 Public Primary Certification Authority - G5

findings.title: PKCS#7 signature verifies cryptographically
findings.description: The primary Authenticode PKCS#7 signature was verified against the signer certificate's public key.

findings.title: Authenticode PE hash matches (binary not tampered)
findings.description: Recomputed sha1 hash of the PE image matches the value embedded in SpcIndirectDataContent.

findings.title: Nested signature(s) present: sha256, sha256, sha256
findings.description: The primary signature is dual-signed — a second (typically SHA-256) signature is nested via MsSpcNestedSignature. Windows on modern Windows 10+ / HVCI prefers the SHA-256 side for verification.

findings.title: Chain anchored to kernel-trusted root (ms-root-referenced)
findings.description: Chain terminates at 'Microsoft Code Verification Root' — recognised as kernel-trusted (ms-root-referenced).

findings.title: HVCI incompatible: missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag
findings.description: This driver is NOT compatible with Hypervisor-protected Code Integrity (HVCI / Memory Integrity). On systems with HVCI enabled, Windows will BLOCK this driver from loading. Issues: missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag.

findings.title: Driver architecture: x64
findings.description: 64-bit driver — loads on x64 Windows

findings.title: Load compatibility: BLOCKED — 1 issue(s) prevent loading
findings.description:
[PASS] Expired + Timestamped: Expired cert but has valid timestamp — signature accepted
  [BLOCKED] HVCI: Blocked when Memory Integrity is ON (missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag)
  [PASS] Secure Boot: Compatible (signed driver)
  [PASS] Timestamp: Expired cert but timestamped by Symantec Time Stamping Services Signer - G4

passes: Signed 2020-09-18 (pre-expiry) — cert expiry ignored by Windows
Counter-sig binds to primary (2020-09-18); legacy TSA crypto — Windows CryptoAPI accepts it
Signed 2020-09-18 (pre-expiry) — cert expiry ignored by Windows
Counter-sig binds to primary (2020-09-18); legacy TSA crypto — Windows CryptoAPI accepts it

blockers: FORCE_INTEGRITY flag not set (HVCI requires it on the PE header)
passes: Signed 2020-09-18 (pre-expiry) — cert expiry ignored by Windows
Counter-sig binds to primary (2020-09-18); legacy TSA crypto — Windows CryptoAPI accepts it
Any valid signature accepted under test-signing

blockers: No WHQL attestation EKU — S Mode / Secured-Core require WHQL-signed drivers
No Authenticode page hashes (Secured-Core requires per-page integrity)

findings.title: Device Access Security Audit
findings.description: Uses IoCreateDevice (not IoCreateDeviceSecure); Missing FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag; Device is not exclusive (multiple handles allowed); Exposed via symlinks: \DosDevices\AMDRyzenMasterDriverV17

findings.title: ROP/JOP gadgets found: 1183 total (misc: 499, memory-read: 269, reg-control: 194, memory-write: 173, jmp-reg: 25, stack-pivot: 23)
findings.description:
Found 1183 usable ROP/JOP gadgets in executable sections. Since many drivers lack ASLR, these gadgets are at predictable addresses and can be chained for kernel exploitation.

Top gadgets by type:
  [stack-pivot] (23 found):
    0x140008922: pop rsp ; and al, 0x30 ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x14000891F: add byte ptr [rax - 0x75], cl ; pop rsp ; and al, 0x30 ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret

findings.title: Exploit Chain: Physical Memory Attack
findings.description:
Difficulty: MEDIUM — requires physical-to-virtual translation
Required primitives: physical-rw

Attack steps:
  1. Use phys-rw (IOCTL 0x81112F08 [phys mem map], 0x81112F1C [phys mem map], 0x81112F5C [phys mem map] +2 more) to scan physical memory for EPROCESS signatures
  2. Locate current process EPROCESS in physical memory
  3. Overwrite Token field with SYSTEM process token
  4. Alternative: map ntoskrnl .text, patch code inline
  5. Spawn SYSTEM shell
